---
title: "Gene-Environment Correlations and Assortative Mating"
subtitle: "ESSGN 2026 Workshop"
author: "Espen Eilertsen & Hans Fredrik Sunde"
date: 2026-03-25
format:
  revealjs:
    embed-resources: true
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    center: false
    chalkboard: false
    smaller: true
    scrollable: true
    transition: fade
    fig-align: center
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.04
    code-fold: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
  cache: true
---


## What does a GWAS estimate?
The infensimal model says that the phenotype is a sum of many small genetic effects plus an environment:
$$
y_i = \sum_{j=1}^{M} \beta_j x_{ij} + e_i
$$

In a GWAS, each SNP $j$ is tested separately in a linear model:

$$
y_i = \alpha_j + \tilde{\beta}_j x_{ij} + \dots + \varepsilon_{ij}
$$


$\tilde{\beta}_j$ is the **marginal coefficient** measuring the ***association*** between $x_{ij}$ and $y_i$: 

$$
\begin{array}{r c c c c c c}
\tilde{\beta}_j
& = &
\beta_j
& + &
\color{red}{\sum_{k \ne j}^{M} \beta_k \frac{\text{Cov}(x_j, x_k)}{\text{Var}(x_j)}}
& + &
\color{blue}{\frac{\text{Cov}(x_j, e)}{\text{Var}(x_j)}} \\
& &
\scriptstyle {\text{Causal Effect}}
& &
\scriptstyle {\text{Linkage Disequilibrium}}
& &
\scriptstyle {\text{rGE}}
\end{array}
$$

## Lecture Outline

The unifying theme today is that different sources of confounding ultimately comes down to the same thing: **marginal associations can be confounded by correlation structure**.

1. **Part 1: Local linkage disequilibrium (LD)**

   Why nearby correlated SNPs both distort and inform marginal effect estimates
2. **Part 2: Assortative mating and long-range LD**

   How mating behavioirs affect correlations between distant loci
3. **Part 3: Gene-environment correlations**

   How indirect effects, stratification, and correlated environments enter SNP estimates and variance decompositions
4. **Part 4: Family-based methods**

   How family designs remove much of the family-level confounding around marginal SNP estimates
5. **Part 5: Practical**

   Estimating SNP heritability and a genetic correlation with LD score regression

# Part 1: Local Linkage Disequilibrium {background-color="#2c3e50" style="color: white;"}

## Nearby SNPs correlate

Recombination shuffles **chromosome segments**, not isolated SNPs. Loci that are close together are less likely to be separated by recombination and therefore tend to be inherited together. 

This makes nearby SNPs correlated in the population: $\text{Cov}(x_j, x_k) \neq 0$ for nearby $j,k$

This is called (local) **linkage disequilibrium (LD)**.


<table style="border-collapse: collapse; font-family: Lato;">
  <tr><td colspan="11" style="height:10px;"></td></tr>
   <tr>
    <td style="border: none; text-align: right;"><b>$t_{grandmother}$</b></td>
    <td style="background:#d6eaf8; border:1px solid #ccc;">1</td>
    <td style="background:#d6eaf8; border:1px solid #ccc;">0</td>
    <td style="background:#d6eaf8; border:1px solid #ccc;">1</td>
    <td style="background:#d6eaf8; border:1px solid #ccc;">1</td>
    <td style="background:#d6eaf8; border:1px solid #ccc;">0</td>
    <td style="background:#d6eaf8; border:1px solid #ccc;">1</td>
    <td style="border:1px solid #ccc;">0</td>
    <td style="border:1px solid #ccc;">0</td>
    <td style="border:1px solid #ccc;">1</td>
    <td style="border:1px solid #ccc;">1</td>
  </tr>

  <tr>
    <td style="border: none; text-align: right;"><b>$t_{grandfather}$</b></td>
    <td style="border:1px solid #ccc;">0</td>
    <td style="border:1px solid #ccc;">1</td>
    <td style="border:1px solid #ccc;">0</td>
    <td style="border:1px solid #ccc;">0</td>
    <td style="border:1px solid #ccc;">1</td>
    <td style="border:1px solid #ccc;">0</td>
    <td style="background:#fadbd8; border:1px solid #ccc;">1</td>
    <td style="background:#fadbd8; border:1px solid #ccc;">1</td>
    <td style="background:#fadbd8; border:1px solid #ccc;">0</td>
    <td style="background:#fadbd8; border:1px solid #ccc;">0</td>
  </tr>

  <tr><td colspan="11" style="height:10px;"></td></tr>

  <tr>
    <td style="border: none; text-align: right;"><b>$t_{parent}$</b></td>
    <td style="background:#d6eaf8; border:1px solid #ccc;"><b>1</b></td>
    <td style="background:#d6eaf8; border:1px solid #ccc;"><b>0</b></td>
    <td style="background:#d6eaf8; border:1px solid #ccc;"><b>1</b></td>
    <td style="background:#d6eaf8; border:1px solid #ccc;"><b>1</b></td>
    <td style="background:#d6eaf8; border:1px solid #ccc;"><b>0</b></td>
    <td style="background:#d6eaf8; border:1px solid #ccc;"><b>1</b></td>
    <td style="background:#fadbd8; border:1px solid #ccc;"><b>1</b></td>
    <td style="background:#fadbd8; border:1px solid #ccc;"><b>1</b></td>
    <td style="background:#fadbd8; border:1px solid #ccc;"><b>0</b></td>
    <td style="background:#fadbd8; border:1px solid #ccc;"><b>0</b></td>
  </tr>
</table>

::: {.callout-note}
If two nearby SNPs are often inherited together, they are statistically *dependent*: once you know the allele at one SNP, you can make a good guess about the allele at the other.
:::


## A two-SNP toy example


Assume the true model is:

$$
y_i = \beta_1 x_{i1} + e_i
$$

where $x_1$ is causal and is in LD with $x_2$, a non-causal SNP:

$$
\beta_1 \neq 0, \qquad \beta_2 = 0, \qquad \text{Cov}(x_1,x_2) \neq 0
$$

A GWAS tests each SNP separately. For $x_2$, the marginal coefficient is:

$$
\tilde{\beta}_2
=
\color{grey}{\beta_2}
\color{grey}{+}
\beta_1 \frac{\text{Cov}(x_2,x_1)}{\text{Var}(x_2)}
$$

In other words, $x_2$ will be associated with the phenotype even though it has **no direct causal effect**.


## Generalizing to many SNPs

Recall that the marginal coefficient at SNP $j$ is (ignoring the rGE term for now):

$$
\tilde{\beta}_j = \beta_j
+ \sum_{k \ne j} \beta_k \frac{\text{Cov}(x_j, x_k)}{\text{Var}(x_j)}
$$

If we standardize the SNPs so that $\text{Var}(x_j)=1$, then $\text{Cov}(x_j,x_k)=r_{jk}$ and this becomes

$$
\tilde{\beta}_j = \sum_{k=1}^M r_{jk}\beta_k
$$

So the marginal coefficient at SNP $j$ is a weighted sum of the direct effects at correlated SNPs (including itself). A SNP that is correlated with **many** nearby variants can therefore pick up more tagged signal.


## What is an LD score?

For SNP $j$, the **LD score** is the amount of local tagging it has:

$$
\ell_j = \sum_{k \in \mathcal{N}(j)} r_{jk}^2
$$

Here $\mathcal{N}(j)$ is a local window around SNP $j$, and the square matters because tagging accumulates in **magnitude**, not in signed direction.

| SNP | Local LD pattern | LD score |
|---|---|---|
| Low-LD SNP | Few or weak local correlations | Small $\ell_j$ |
| High-LD SNP | Many strong local correlations | Large $\ell_j$ |

::: {.callout-note}
A high LD score means the SNP is highly correlated with many nearby variants, and is more likely to tag causal variation in the region. 

A low LD score means the SNP is more isolated and less likely to tag nearby causal effects.
:::

## LD score regression {.smaller}

If SNPs with higher LD scores tag more nearby causal variation, then under polygenicity their test statistics should be larger on average. How much larger depends on the heritability:

$$
E[\chi_j^2] \approx 1 + \frac{N h_g^2}{M}\ell_j + c
$$

where $\chi_j^2 = z_j^2$, and $z_j = \hat{\tilde{\beta}}_j/\text{SE}(\hat{\tilde{\beta}}_j)$ for SNP $j$.

::: {.callout-note}
## High-level interpretation
- Regress GWAS test statistics on LD scores
- Because the expected test statistic rises systematically with local LD, we can exploit that relationship to estimate SNP heritability
- The **slope** tracks polygenic signal, but it must be rescaled to get heritability:
  $h_g^2 = \text{slope} \times M / N$
- The **intercept** tracks confounding or other inflation

Other considerations include  appropriate regression weights to handle LD-dependent redundancy, plus a well-matched LD reference panel, careful GWAS QC, and checking for sample overlap or ancestry mismatch.
:::

## Genetic correlation from cross-trait LDSC {.smaller}

For two traits, the same idea extends to the product of the two marginal $z$-statistics:

$$
E[z_{1j}z_{2j}]
\approx
\frac{\sqrt{N_1N_2}}{M} r_g \sqrt{h_{g1}^2 h_{g2}^2}\,\ell_j + c_{12}
$$

where $r_g$ is the **genetic correlation**, summarizing how strongly the additive genetic effects overlap across the two traits.

::: {.callout-note}
## High-level interpretation
- Regress cross-trait $z$-score products on LD scores
- The slope tracks **shared genetic signal** across the two traits, but it first has to be rescaled to get the genetic covariance:
  $\mathrm{Cov}(g_1,g_2) = \text{slope} \times M / \sqrt{N_1N_2}$
- Then standardize that covariance to get the genetic correlation:
  $r_g = \mathrm{Cov}(g_1,g_2) / \sqrt{h_{g1}^2 h_{g2}^2}$
:::

## Summary: local LD both confounds and helps

| Step | Main lesson |
|---|---|
| Nearby SNPs correlate | Recombination makes nearby loci travel together |
| Marginal coefficients absorb tagged effects | So $\tilde{\beta}_j$ need not equal $\beta_j$ |
| LD scores quantify local tagging | They summarize how much nearby variation a SNP captures |
| LD score regression exploits that structure | The same LD that complicates interpretation can be modeled |

::: {.callout-note}
This is the template for the rest of the lecture: **marginal associations are projections through correlation structure**.
:::

# Part 2: Assortative Mating and Long-Range LD {background-color="#2c3e50" style="color: white;"}

## What is assortative mating?

Partner similarity, $\text{Cov}(y_m, y_p) > 0$, can arise for different reasons. 

**Assortative mating (AM)** means that the **matching process itself** induces covariance between partners, rather than that covariance reflecting a shared cause. 

::: {.callout-note}
Observed partner covariance is a **symptom**, not yet an explanation.

- **Assortative mating**: matching induces partner covariance
- **Convergence**: partners become more similar after pairing
- **Stratification**: partners are similar because both are shaped by the same background factors

Here, we assume that partner covariance is due to assortative mating.
:::


## Assortment makes correlation flow "backwards"

**Ordinary family resemblance**: Covariance starts in the **causes** and is diluted as it shows up in the phenotype (correlation flows from $g$ to $y$).

**Assortative mating**: Covariance starts on the **phenotype**. If partners are similar in $y$, then part of that covariance gets projected back into the causes (correlation flows from $y$ to $g$).

Assortative mating therefore induces genetic similarity between partners.


::: {.callout-note}
## Useful intuition
Assortative mating induces correlations among causes by matching on the effects. 
:::


## Why regressing $g$ on $y$ gives $h^2$ {.smaller}
To quantify the genetic similarity, we temporarily place ourselves at the **outcome** and ask: if we observe someone's phenotype $y$, what genetic value $g$ would we predict? 

We start with the simple additive model:

$$
y_i = g_i + e_i,
\qquad
\text{Cov}(g_i,e_i)=0
\qquad \Rightarrow \qquad
\text{Cov}(g_i,y_i)
= \text{Var}(g_i)
= \sigma_g^2
$$

So the linear projection of $g$ on $y$ has slope

$$
b_{g \mid y}
= \frac{\text{Cov}(g,y)}{\text{Var}(y)}
= \frac{\sigma_g^2}{\sigma_y^2}
= h^2
$$

::: {.callout-note}
If $\text{Cov}(g,e) \neq 0$, this projection coefficient is still $\text{Cov}(g,y)/\text{Var}(y)$, but it is no longer exactly narrow-sense heritability. We set that complication aside here.
:::

## Matching on $y$ induces covariance in $g$ {.smaller}

If partners match on phenotype, then project back into $g$ on **both** sides:

$$
\hat{g}_m = h^2 y_m,
\qquad
\hat{g}_f = h^2 y_f
$$

Therefore

$$
\text{Cov}(g_m,g_f)
=
h^4 \text{Cov}(y_m,y_f)
$$

Divide both sides by $\sigma_g^2$ to convert the left-hand side to a genetic correlation:

$$
r_g^{\mathrm{mates}}
=
\frac{\text{Cov}(g_m,g_f)}{\sigma_g^2}
\approx
\frac{(\sigma_g^2/\sigma_y^2)^2\text{Cov}(y_m,y_f)}{\sigma_g^2}
=
\frac{\sigma_g^2}{\sigma_y^2}
\frac{\text{Cov}(y_m,y_f)}{\sigma_y^2}
= h^2 r_y^{\mathrm{mates}}
$$

::: {.callout-note}
The $h^4$ appears on the covariance scale because the phenotype-to-genotype projection ($\sigma_g^2/\sigma_y^2 = h^2$) happens **once for each mate**. When we convert covariance to correlation, one power of $\sigma_g^2$ is divided back out, leaving the simpler result $r_g^{\mathrm{mates}} \approx h^2 r_y^{\mathrm{mates}}$. 
:::


## Direct versus indirect assortment {.smaller}

It helps to distinguish the **focal phenotype** $y$ from the **sorting factor** $s$ that partners actually match on.

| Case | What partners match on | Induced genotypic correlation between partners |
|--|----|---|
| **Direct assortment** | The focal phenotype itself: $s = y$ | $r_g^{\mathrm{mates}} \approx \mathrm{Corr}(g,y)^2 r_y^{\mathrm{mates}} = h^2 r_y^{\mathrm{mates}}$ |
| **Indirect assortment** | A related signal or bundle of traits: $s \neq y$, but $\mathrm{Corr}(s,y) \neq 0$ | $r_g^{\mathrm{mates}} \approx \mathrm{Corr}(g,s)^2 r_s^{\mathrm{mates}}$ |

::: {.callout-important}
The key point is that the **genotypic correlation between partners depends on how strongly the sorting factor $s$ correlate with the genetic causes of the focal phenotype**. So indirect assortment can imply **more or less** genetic resemblance between partners than you would expect from the observed partner correlation in $y$ alone.

In this lecture, we assume **direct-assortment** where $s=y$, but note that assortative mating is indirect for many social traits (e.g., educational attainment).
:::

## Mate-level genetic covariance is a sum of SNP-level covariance {.smaller}

Write the genetic value as a weighted sum of SNPs:$g_i = \sum_{j=1}^{M} \beta_j x_{ij}$. Then partner genetic covariance expands as

$$
\text{Cov}(g_m,g_f)
=
\text{Cov}\!\left(\underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{M} \beta_j x_{mj}}_{g_m}, \underbrace{\sum_{k=1}^{M} \beta_k x_{fk}}_{g_f}\right)
=
\sum_{j=1}^{M}\sum_{k=1}^{M}
 \,\text{Cov}(\beta_j x_{mj}, \beta_k x_{fk})
$$

So mate-level genetic covariance is the weighted sum of many cross-mate SNP covariances. Phenotype assortment can therefore induce many small SNP-level correlations across partners.

::: {.callout-note}
This is the bridge to long-range LD: once partner genomes become correlated, transmission and recombination can move that covariance into the next generation.
:::

## One generation creates trans covariance first {.smaller}

To match Session 1, let $\mathbf{t}_m = (t_{m1},\ldots,t_{mM})^\top$ and $\mathbf{t}_p = (t_{p1},\ldots,t_{pM})^\top$ denote the transmitted maternal and paternal haplotype vectors.

$$
\mathbf{t}_m + \mathbf{t}_p = \mathbf{x}_c,
\qquad
\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{t}_m = g_c^{(m)},
\qquad
\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{t}_p = g_c^{(p)},
\qquad
g_c = g_c^{(m)} + g_c^{(p)}
$$

If we focus on the **new** covariance induced by assortative mating and set aside ordinary local LD from Part 1, then after one generation the new covariance is across the two parental haplotypes:

$$
\text{Cov}(g_c^{(m)}, g_c^{(p)}) > 0
$$

but not yet within either transmitted haplotype, so

$$
\text{Var}(g_c^{(m)}) \approx \sum_{j=1}^{M} \beta_j^2 \text{Var}(t_{mj}),
\qquad
\text{Var}(g_c^{(p)}) \approx \sum_{j=1}^{M} \beta_j^2 \text{Var}(t_{pj})
$$

Therefore

$$
\text{Var}(g_c)
=
\text{Var}(g_c^{(m)})
+ \text{Var}(g_c^{(p)})
+ 2\text{Cov}(g_c^{(m)}, g_c^{(p)})
$$

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}
```{dot}
//| fig-width: 4.6
//| fig-height: 3
//| echo: false
digraph trans_cov {
  rankdir=TB;
  graph [margin=0.08, nodesep=0.5, ranksep=0.7];
  node [shape=circle, style=filled, fixedsize=true, width=0.85, height=0.85,
        fontsize=17, fontcolor=white, fontname="Helvetica-Bold", penwidth=2];
  edge [penwidth=2, fontsize=15, fontname="Helvetica-Bold"];

  gm [label="g_c^(m)", fillcolor="#3498db", color="#2980b9"];
  gf [label="g_c^(p)", fillcolor="#e74c3c", color="#c0392b"];
  go [label="g_c", fillcolor="#7f8c8d", color="#707b7c"];

  gm -> go;
  gf -> go;
  gm -> gf [dir=none, style=dashed, color="#27ae60", label=" trans cov"];
}
```
:::
::: {.column width="55%"}

This is **trans** covariance: covariance across the maternal and paternal transmitted genetic contributions induced by the haplotypes $\mathbf{t}_m$ and $\mathbf{t}_p$, before recombination has had time to create new within-haplotype covariance.

:::
::::

::: {.callout-note}
Here, "trans" and "cis" are bookkeeping terms for **where the covariance lives**. They are not new biological mechanisms.
:::

## Later generations build cis covariance and long-range LD {.smaller}

Repeated assortment plus recombination gradually converts part of the accumulated trans covariance into **cis** covariance within transmitted haplotypes.

That means the haplotype variance is no longer just a sum of single-locus terms. For example,

$$
\text{Var}(g_c^{(m)})
=
\sum_{j=1}^{M}\sum_{k=1}^{M}
\text{Cov}(\beta_j t_{mj}, \beta_k t_{mk})
=
\sum_{j=1}^{M} \beta_j^2 \text{Var}(t_{mj})
+ 2\sum_{j<k}\beta_j\beta_k\text{Cov}(t_{mj}, t_{mk})
$$

and similarly for $g_c^{(p)}$. The off-diagonal terms are the new **cis** covariance terms.

Once those within-haplotype covariances become nonzero for distant loci, assortative mating has generated long-range LD:

$$
\text{Cov}(t_{mj}, t_{mk}) \neq 0
\qquad
\text{for distant loci } j,k
$$

| Where the covariance lives | When it appears | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Trans covariance | First generation | Maternal and paternal contributions become aligned |
| Cis covariance | Later generations | Aligned alleles begin to co-occur within haplotypes |
| Long-range LD | Repeated assortment | Distant loci become correlated in the population |

::: {.callout-note}
Assortative mating does not just create "more LD". It changes the **geography** of LD by creating nonlocal correlation structure.
:::

## Why marginal SNP effects now tag distant variants {.smaller}

Again set the rGE term aside and focus on the LD component:

$$
\tilde{\beta}_j
= \beta_j
+ \sum_{k \ne j} \beta_k \frac{\text{Cov}(x_j, x_k)}{\text{Var}(x_j)}
$$

Under local LD, the sum is dominated by nearby loci. Under assortative mating, some of those covariance terms can involve **distant** causal variants.

::: {.callout-note}
So a marginal hit at SNP $j$ can partly reflect genome-wide covariance structure, not only local biology at SNP $j$.
:::

## Why $\text{Var}(g)$ increases for a few generations {.smaller}

Using the haplotype decomposition,

$$
\text{Var}(g_c)
=
\text{Var}(g_c^{(m)})
+ \text{Var}(g_c^{(p)})
+ 2\text{Cov}(g_c^{(m)}, g_c^{(p)})
$$

After one generation, the increase comes mainly from the positive **trans** term $2\text{Cov}(g_c^{(m)},g_c^{(p)})$. After later generations, each haplotype contribution also contains positive **cis** terms:

$$
\text{Var}(g_c^{(m)})
=
\sum_{j=1}^{M} \beta_j^2 \text{Var}(t_{mj})
+ 2\sum_{j<k}\beta_j\beta_k\text{Cov}(t_{mj},t_{mk})
$$

and similarly for $g_c^{(p)}$.

| Generation | Main covariance pattern | Expected $\text{Var}(g)$ |
|---|---|---|
| Random mating baseline | Little long-range covariance | Baseline |
| After 1 generation | Positive trans covariance | Higher |
| After several generations | Trans + cis covariance | Higher still |
| Equilibrium | Assortment balanced by recombination | Plateau |

::: {.callout-note}
This trajectory is schematic: positive assortment tends to increase genetic variance for a few generations and then approach a new equilibrium, not increase forever.
:::

## Relatives also resemble each other more

Once parental genomes are more aligned, resemblance among relatives increases for the trait under assortment.

If mates are positively correlated in genetic value, then:

$$
\text{Cov}(g_m, g_f) > 0
$$

and therefore:

$$
\text{Var}\!\left(\frac{g_m + g_f}{2}\right)
= \frac{1}{4}\text{Var}(g_m)
+ \frac{1}{4}\text{Var}(g_f)
+ \frac{1}{2}\text{Cov}(g_m, g_f)
$$

::: {.callout-note}
The same induced covariance structure now appears in two places:
- in GWAS, through long-range tagging
- in families, through stronger resemblance among relatives
:::

## Summary: assortative mating changes GWAS, LD, and family resemblance

| Step | Main lesson |
|---|---|
| Mates match on phenotype | Start with $\text{Cov}(y_m,y_f) > 0$ |
| First generation | Mostly trans covariance appears |
| Later generations | Cis covariance and long-range LD build up |
| Population consequence | $\text{Var}(g)$ rises for a while, then approaches equilibrium |
| Family consequence | Relatives resemble each other more |

::: {.callout-note}
Again, marginal associations reflect **induced correlation structure**, not only direct causal effects.
:::

# Part 3: Gene-Environment Correlations {background-color="#2c3e50" style="color: white;"}

## Same projection, new covariance sources

Now set the LD term aside and focus on gene-environment correlation:

$$
\tilde{\beta}_j = \beta_j + \frac{\text{Cov}(x_j, e)}{\text{Var}(x_j)}
$$

Part 3 is about a new question:

$$
\text{What can generate } \text{Cov}(x_j, e)?
$$

::: {.callout-note}
In Part 1 and Part 2, the extra terms came mainly from **correlated genotypes**. Here the focus is **correlated environments**.
:::

## Where can $\text{Cov}(x_j,e)$ come from?

| Source | Why genotype and environment become correlated |
|---|---|
| Passive rGE | Parents transmit both genes and family environments |
| Evocative / active rGE | Genotypes shape responses from others or environmental choices |
| Indirect genetic effects | Other people's genotypes shape the focal person's environment |
| Stratification / population structure | Allele frequencies and environments differ across groups |
| Residual confounding | Selection, ascertainment, measurement, or omitted structure |

::: {.callout-note}
Different mechanisms can produce the **same statistical symptom** in a marginal GWAS coefficient: $\text{Cov}(x_j,e) \neq 0$.
:::

## Indirect genetic effects

A focal person's phenotype can be affected not only by their own genotype, but also by the genotypes of parents, siblings, peers, or partners through the environments those people create.

$$
y_i = g_i^{\text{direct}} + g_i^{\text{indirect}} + e_i
\qquad
g_i^{\text{direct}} = \sum_{j=1}^{M}\beta_j x_{ij}
$$

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{dot}
//| fig-width: 5
//| fig-height: 3.1
//| echo: false
digraph ige {
  rankdir=LR;
  graph [margin=0.08, nodesep=0.45, ranksep=0.7];
  node [shape=circle, style=filled, fixedsize=true, width=0.85, height=0.85,
        fontsize=17, fontcolor=white, fontname="Helvetica-Bold", penwidth=2];
  edge [penwidth=2, fontsize=15, fontname="Helvetica-Bold"];

  xr [label="x_rel", fillcolor="#e74c3c", color="#c0392b"];
  env [label="env", fillcolor="#e67e22", color="#ca6f1e"];
  xi [label="x_i", fillcolor="#3498db", color="#2980b9"];
  yi [label="y_i", fillcolor="#7f8c8d", color="#707b7c"];

  xr -> env [label=" indirect"];
  env -> yi [label=" environment"];
  xi -> yi [label=" direct"];
}
```
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

- Your own genotype can affect your phenotype directly
- Other people's genotypes can affect your phenotype indirectly by shaping your environment
- That is why indirect genetic effects are not the same thing as stratification

:::
::::

## Stratification and population structure

Stratification can also induce correlation between genotype and environment, but through group-level differences rather than through other people's genotypes creating the focal environment.

```{dot}
//| fig-width: 5.2
//| fig-height: 3
//| echo: false
digraph strat {
  rankdir=TB;
  graph [margin=0.08, nodesep=0.5, ranksep=0.7];
  node [shape=circle, style=filled, fixedsize=true, width=0.9, height=0.9,
        fontsize=17, fontcolor=white, fontname="Helvetica-Bold", penwidth=2];
  edge [penwidth=2, fontsize=15, fontname="Helvetica-Bold"];

  anc [label="group", fillcolor="#8e44ad", color="#7d3c98"];
  xj  [label="x_j", fillcolor="#3498db", color="#2980b9"];
  e   [label="e", fillcolor="#e67e22", color="#ca6f1e"];
  y   [label="y", fillcolor="#7f8c8d", color="#707b7c"];

  anc -> xj;
  anc -> e;
  xj -> y [label=" marginal"];
  e  -> y [label=" env"];
}
```

::: {.callout-important}
Stratification is **confounding**, not nurture. It can create $\text{Cov}(x_j,e)$ even when there is no within-family environmental pathway from genotype to phenotype.
:::

## Back to the marginal SNP coefficient {.smaller}

For this part, the relevant simplification is:

$$
\tilde{\beta}_j
= \beta_j + \frac{\text{Cov}(x_j, e)}{\text{Var}(x_j)}
$$

::: {.callout-note}
The orange term can now reflect many different things:
- passive, evocative, or active gene-environment correlation
- indirect genetic effects from parents, siblings, peers, or partners
- stratification and population structure
- other residual confounding
:::

## From SNP confounding to variance decomposition

For the next two slides, let $g$ denote the focal individual's **direct additive genetic value**:

$$
g_i = \sum_{j=1}^{M} \beta_j x_{ij}
$$

Then:

$$
y = g + e
\qquad \Longrightarrow \qquad
\text{Var}(y) = \text{Var}(g) + \text{Var}(e) + 2\text{Cov}(g,e)
$$

::: {.callout-note}
If you split indirect genetic effects out as their own term, you get more covariance terms, not fewer. The interpretational problem does not disappear.
:::

## Why covariance terms complicate interpretation

Keep these quantities conceptually separate:

$$
\text{Var}(g) \qquad \text{Var}(e) \qquad \text{Cov}(g,e) \qquad \text{Var}(y)
$$

The sign of the covariance matters:

| Sign of $\text{Cov}(g,e)$ | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| Positive | Genetically higher-valued individuals tend to experience environments that push in the same direction |
| Negative | Genetically higher-valued individuals tend to experience environments that push in the opposite direction |

::: {.callout-important}
$\text{Var}(y) = \text{Var}(g) + \text{Var}(e) + 2\text{Cov}(g,e)$ is a **statistical identity**, not a clean causal partition of phenotypic variation.
:::

## Take-home map

| Quantity | What it means | What it does not automatically mean |
|---|---|---|
| Direct effect $\beta_j$ | Causal effect of locus $j$ in the full model | The same thing as the marginal coefficient |
| Marginal coefficient $\tilde{\beta}_j$ | Association between SNP $j$ and phenotype | A pure direct effect |
| Indirect genetic effect | Other people's genotypes shape the focal environment | Population stratification |
| Stratification | Group structure aligns genotypes and environments | Genetic nurture |
| $\text{Cov}(g,e)$ | Statistical alignment of genetic values and environments | A causal share of variance |

::: {.callout-note}
Across local LD, assortative mating, and gene-environment correlation, the same lesson keeps returning: **marginal associations are projections through correlation structure**.
:::

# Part 4: Family GWAS {background-color="#2c3e50" style="color: white;"}

## Why families help

Much of this lecture has been about the fact that the **population** coefficient $\tilde{\beta}_j$ can include more than the direct effect $\beta_j$.

If we compare unrelated people, that coefficient can still reflect LD, assortative mating, stratification, genetic nurture, and other family-level environments.

::: {.callout-note}
Family GWAS changes the comparison. Instead of relying only on differences across unrelated families, it uses variation **within families** to get closer to the direct effect.
:::

## A family-based regression

Index individuals by $i$ within family $f$. A simple within-family model is:

$$
y_{if} = \alpha_f + \tilde{\beta}_j^{F} x_{ijf} + \varepsilon_{if}
$$

where $\alpha_f$ is a family intercept shared by relatives in the family design.

With two siblings, this is equivalent to differencing:

$$
y_{1f} - y_{2f}
= \tilde{\beta}_j^{F}\left(x_{1jf} - x_{2jf}\right)
+ \left(\varepsilon_{1f} - \varepsilon_{2f}\right)
$$

::: {.callout-note}
This is exactly where Session 1's **segregation variance** comes back in. The sibling difference $x_{1jf} - x_{2jf}$ is generated by the random segregation deviations in transmission, not by between-family differences in ancestry or parental background.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
Sibling differencing is only **one example** of a family design. The next day's session will go deeper into family-based GWAS more broadly.
:::

## Comparing the two coefficients

| Estimate | What variation identifies it | What it tends to pick up |
|---|---|---|
| Population coefficient $\tilde{\beta}_j$ | Differences across individuals and families | Direct effects plus between-family confounding and tagging structure |
| Family-design coefficient $\tilde{\beta}_j^{F}$ | Segregation-driven differences within families | Often closer to the direct effect $\beta_j$ |

::: {.callout-note}
The point is not that $\tilde{\beta}_j^{F}$ is magically perfect. The point is that it removes much of the **between-family** structure that biases the population coefficient.
:::

## Why this solves many earlier problems

Relatives in the same family share parents, ancestry, and much of their family environment. That means many family-level confounders are held fixed by the comparison.

| Earlier problem | Why family GWAS helps |
|---|---|
| Stratification / population structure | Relatives in the same family share the same family ancestry background |
| Passive rGE / parental genetic nurture | Parental genotypes and much of the rearing environment are common within families |
| Assortative mating background | The parental genomes are the same for the compared relatives |
| Shared family environment | The family intercept absorbs what relatives share |

::: {.callout-note}
So $\tilde{\beta}_j^{F}$ is often **closer to the direct effect** than the population-level marginal coefficient.
:::

## What it does not fully solve

Family GWAS solves **a lot**, but not everything.

| Remaining issue | Why it can remain |
|---|---|
| Local LD | Relatives still inherit correlated nearby variants |
| Indirect genetic effects | One relative's genotype can affect another relative's environment |
| Within-family environmental differences | Family members do not experience identical environments |
| Lower power | Within-family genotype differences are smaller than population-wide variation |

::: {.callout-important}
Family GWAS is best thought of as a design that removes much of the **between-family confounding** around $\tilde{\beta}_j$. It does not magically make every estimate equal the direct causal effect.
:::

## Summary: family GWAS gets closer to direct effects

| Comparison | Main source of identifying variation |
|---|---|
| Population GWAS | Differences across people, families, and environments |
| Family GWAS | Mendelian segregation across relatives within the same family |

::: {.callout-note}
This is why family GWAS is such a useful complement to the rest of the lecture: it directly targets many of the confounding mechanisms we have been discussing, and tomorrow's session will go deeper into the design details.
:::

# Part 5: Practical {background-color="#2c3e50" style="color: white;"}

## Practical 2: LD score regression with real GWAS data

In this practical, we will use **LD score regression (LDSC)** to estimate:

1. observed-scale SNP heritability for **intelligence**
2. observed-scale SNP heritability for **depression**
3. the genetic correlation between the two traits

The summary statistics come from the **CTG summary statistics archive** and the software is the canonical **LDSC** pipeline used in many real analyses:

- [CTG GWAS summary statistics](https://ctg.thebluebus.nl/software/summary_statistics/)
- [LDSC repository](https://github.com/bulik/ldsc)
- [LDSC heritability and genetic correlation tutorial](https://github.com/bulik/ldsc/wiki/Heritability-and-Genetic-Correlation)

::: {.callout-note}
To keep the exercise focused, the instructor has already downloaded the public files, standardized the columns, and prepared course-ready inputs. You will still run the same LDSC commands used in practice: `munge_sumstats.py`, single-trait `--h2`, and cross-trait `--rg`.
:::

## Setup and inputs {.smaller}

We will work with two pre-harmonized files prepared from the public CTG releases:

- `practical/ldsc/data/course/intelligence_ctg.tsv.gz`
- `practical/ldsc/data/course/depression_ctg.tsv.gz`

Each file has the same minimal schema:

`SNP`, `A1`, `A2`, `P`, `N`, `Z`

We also need the LDSC reference files:

- `practical/ldsc/data/ref/w_hm3.snplist`
- `practical/ldsc/data/ref/eur_w_ld_chr/`
- `practical/ldsc/data/ref/weights_hm3_no_hla/`

```bash
export LDSC_DIR=/path/to/ldsc
mkdir -p practical/ldsc/data/results
```

::: {.callout-note}
These LD scores are based on **1000 Genomes Europeans**, so the exercise assumes European-ancestry GWAS summary statistics. In real work, ancestry mismatch between the GWAS and the reference panel can bias interpretation.
:::

## Part 1: Munge the summary statistics {.smaller}

First convert the prepared text files into LDSC's `.sumstats.gz` format.

```bash
python "$LDSC_DIR/munge_sumstats.py" \
  --sumstats practical/ldsc/data/course/intelligence_ctg.tsv.gz \
  --out practical/ldsc/data/results/intelligence_ctg \
  --merge-alleles practical/ldsc/data/ref/w_hm3.snplist

python "$LDSC_DIR/munge_sumstats.py" \
  --sumstats practical/ldsc/data/course/depression_ctg.tsv.gz \
  --out practical/ldsc/data/results/depression_ctg \
  --merge-alleles practical/ldsc/data/ref/w_hm3.snplist
```

Look for:

- how many SNPs survive QC and allele matching
- whether the log files contain warnings
- the two output files `*.sumstats.gz`

## Part 2: Estimate SNP heritability {.smaller}

Now run single-trait LDSC for each phenotype.

```bash
python "$LDSC_DIR/ldsc.py" \
  --h2 practical/ldsc/data/results/intelligence_ctg.sumstats.gz \
  --ref-ld-chr practical/ldsc/data/ref/eur_w_ld_chr/ \
  --w-ld-chr practical/ldsc/data/ref/weights_hm3_no_hla/weights.hm3_noMHC. \
  --out practical/ldsc/data/results/intelligence_h2

python "$LDSC_DIR/ldsc.py" \
  --h2 practical/ldsc/data/results/depression_ctg.sumstats.gz \
  --ref-ld-chr practical/ldsc/data/ref/eur_w_ld_chr/ \
  --w-ld-chr practical/ldsc/data/ref/weights_hm3_no_hla/weights.hm3_noMHC. \
  --out practical/ldsc/data/results/depression_h2
```

Look for these lines in each log:

- `Total Observed scale h2`
- `Intercept`
- `Ratio`

::: {.callout-note}
For this short practical we stay on the **observed scale**. We are not doing liability-scale conversion or partitioned heritability here.
:::

## Part 3: Estimate the genetic correlation {.smaller}

Finally, estimate the cross-trait genetic correlation.

```bash
python "$LDSC_DIR/ldsc.py" \
  --rg practical/ldsc/data/results/intelligence_ctg.sumstats.gz,practical/ldsc/data/results/depression_ctg.sumstats.gz \
  --ref-ld-chr practical/ldsc/data/ref/eur_w_ld_chr/ \
  --w-ld-chr practical/ldsc/data/ref/weights_hm3_no_hla/weights.hm3_noMHC. \
  --out practical/ldsc/data/results/intelligence_depression_rg
```

Look for:

- `Genetic Correlation`
- its standard error
- the cross-trait intercept

For these traits, we expect a **negative** genetic correlation.

## Interpretation checkpoint

After the run, check that you can answer these questions:

1. Which trait has the larger observed-scale SNP heritability?
2. Are the LDSC intercepts close to 1, or do they suggest substantial inflation beyond polygenicity?
3. Is the estimated genetic correlation negative, and how large is it in magnitude?
4. Is the cross-trait intercept close to 0, or could sample overlap / shared confounding be contributing?

::: {.callout-important}
The important habit is not just to report `h^2` and `r_g`, but also to inspect the **intercepts** and ask whether the analysis assumptions look reasonable.
:::
